lessons.title: Building Your AI Content Stack
lessons.title_es: Construyendo tu stack de contenido con IA
lessons.description: Choose and connect the right AI tools to create a content production system that runs 24/7.
lessons.description_es: Elige y conecta las herramientas de IA adecuadas para crear un sistema de producción de contenido que funcione 24/7.
lessons.content:
The Four-Layer Content Stack

Most businesses treat AI content tools as isolated point solutions — they generate a blog post here, a social caption there. The result is inconsistent brand voice, redundant effort, and no compounding advantage. A proper AI content stack is a layered pipeline where output from one layer feeds the next automatically.

The four layers are:

  • Research layer: Tools like Perplexity, Claude with web access, or SparkToro pull topical signals — what your audience is searching, what competitors are publishing, what questions AI assistants are answering about your niche.
  • Generation layer: A primary LLM (Claude Sonnet for long-form, Haiku for bulk short-form) produces first drafts from structured briefs. The brief is the quality lever — garbage in, garbage out.
  • Refinement layer: Brand voice consistency checks, SEO optimization passes (Surfer, Clearscope, or a custom GPT trained on your top 10 articles), and fact-verification steps run automatically before anything is approved.
  • Distribution layer: Automated publishing to CMS, social scheduling (Buffer, Publer), email drip enrollment, and repurposing triggers. A single approved article automatically spawns 5–7 derivative assets.

The Content Brief Template That Scales

The single biggest leverage point in AI content production is the brief. A weak brief produces weak output that requires heavy editing — negating the speed advantage. A strong brief takes 10 minutes to fill and produces a usable 80% draft every time.

Your standard brief must include:

  • Primary keyword + 3 secondary keywords with current search volume
  • Target reader persona (not demographics — the specific job-to-be-done they have right now)
  • Competitor URLs to beat (3–5 URLs from page 1 that you want to outrank)
  • Unique angle — what this piece argues that the top results do not say
  • Required citations — data points, studies, or expert quotes to include for credibility
  • CTA and next step — what should the reader do when they finish reading

Store briefs in a structured database (Notion, Airtable, or a simple spreadsheet). This becomes your content queue — the system pulls the next brief, runs the generation chain, and returns a draft for human review. The human's job shrinks to quality control, not creation.

Connecting the Tools: Automation Patterns

Use Make (formerly Integromat) or n8n to wire the layers together. A typical workflow: Airtable row moves to "Brief Ready" status → webhook fires → Claude API generates draft → Google Doc created with draft → reviewer notified in Slack. Approval moves the row to "Approved" → CMS post created as draft → social repurposing chain triggered. This workflow runs unattended. Your team reviews and approves; they no longer create from scratch.

lessons.title: Content Repurposing at Scale
lessons.description: Turn one approved piece of content into 8–12 derivative assets automatically.
lessons.description_es: Convierte un artículo aprobado en 8–12 activos derivados de forma automática.
lessons.content:
The Repurposing Multiplier Framework

Every piece of long-form content you produce contains enough material for a week of multi-channel distribution. The problem isn't content volume — it's extraction. AI makes systematic extraction fast enough to run as a standard post-approval step rather than a special project.

From one 1,500-word blog post, a well-configured repurposing chain produces:

  • 3 LinkedIn posts — one for each major section, each reframed as a standalone insight
  • 1 email newsletter section — 200-word summary with the key takeaway and link
  • 5–7 short-form social captions — pull quotes and statistics formatted for Instagram and Facebook
  • 1 script outline for a 5-minute video — talking points extracted in presentation order
  • 1 FAQ page section — the article's implicit questions surfaced and answered directly
  • 1 slide deck outline — headline + 3 bullets per slide, ready for Canva or PowerPoint

Configuring the Extraction Prompts

Repurposing quality depends on prompts that understand format constraints. Generic summarization prompts produce generic output. Build format-specific extraction prompts for each asset type.

LinkedIn post prompt pattern: "Extract the single most counterintuitive insight from the article below. Rewrite it as a LinkedIn post: 3-sentence hook, 5-bullet elaboration, 1-sentence close with a soft CTA. No hashtags. Maintain the author's direct, practitioner voice. Article: [ARTICLE]"

The key parameters to enforce per format:

  • Character limits: Twitter/X 280, LinkedIn 3,000 optimal, Instagram caption 125 before fold
  • Voice calibration: Include 3 example posts from the target channel in your prompt as style anchors
  • CTA variation: Rotate between "save this," "what's your experience," and no CTA to avoid pattern fatigue
  • Platform-native formatting: LinkedIn uses line breaks for breathing room; Twitter uses thread numbering; email uses plain sentences, no bullets in the preview text

Building the Repurposing Queue

Treat repurposing as a production queue, not a one-off task. Every approved article gets tagged with repurposing status in your content database. Set a target of 48-hour turnaround from article approval to all derivatives published or scheduled. Track which derivative formats drive the most traffic back to the original — double down on those. Over 90 days, you'll have empirical data on your highest-leverage repurposing formats, and you can cut the ones that don't move the needle.

lessons.title: Maintaining Brand Voice Across AI Output
lessons.title_es: Mantener la voz de marca en el contenido generado por IA
lessons.description: Build a brand voice document and prompt system that keeps every AI output on-brand without manual rewriting.
lessons.description_es: Crea un documento de voz de marca y un sistema de prompts que mantenga todo el contenido de IA alineado sin reescrituras manuales.
lessons.content:
Why AI Output Sounds Generic (and How to Fix It)

AI models default to the statistical center of all training data — which means polished, professional, and completely forgettable. The antidote is a structured brand voice document that feeds directly into every generation prompt. This is not a marketing document for your website; it's a machine-readable specification for your AI toolchain.

Building the Brand Voice Specification

Your brand voice spec has five components:

  • Voice adjectives (3–5 only): Choose adjectives that are specific enough to be testable. "Professional" is useless. "Blunt, data-first, zero-buzzword" is actionable. Each adjective must have a "this vs. not this" example sentence pair.
  • Vocabulary allowlist and blocklist: Specific words your brand uses (and words it never uses). Example blocklist for a technical B2B brand: "leverage," "synergy," "unlock," "empower," "delve." Example allowlist: precise verbs — "run," "measure," "cut," "build."
  • Sentence structure rules: Average sentence length, maximum sentence length, rules about passive voice (ban it or allow it in specific contexts), rules about rhetorical questions.
  • Paragraph rhythm: How long are your paragraphs? Do you use single-sentence paragraphs for emphasis? Do you open with conclusions and explain, or build to conclusions?
  • 5 canonical examples: Your 5 best-performing pieces of content. These go into the system prompt as style anchors — the model produces output stylistically consistent with these examples.

The System Prompt Integration Pattern

Every generation prompt in your stack must include a voice block. Keep it under 200 tokens to avoid diluting the actual content instruction. The pattern: "You are writing in the voice of [Brand]. Voice: [3 adjectives]. Never use: [blocklist]. Structure: [2–3 rules]. Match the style of these examples: [Example 1] [Example 2]."

Run a voice consistency check as a separate pass — send the generated draft to Claude with the instruction: "Score this draft from 1–10 on each of these voice dimensions: [adjectives]. Flag any sentences that violate the blocklist or structure rules." Use scores below 7 as automatic revision triggers. Over time, track your average voice score per content type. Declining scores signal prompt drift and need correction.

lessons.title: Measuring Content Engine ROI
lessons.title_es: Midiendo el ROI del motor de contenido
lessons.description: Track the metrics that connect AI content production to pipeline and revenue.
lessons.description_es: Rastrea las métricas que conectan la producción de contenido con IA al pipeline y los ingresos.
lessons.content:
The Content ROI Measurement Framework

Content teams fail to demonstrate ROI not because their content doesn't work, but because they measure the wrong things. Impressions and pageviews are activity metrics. The metrics that get budget approved are business outcome metrics: leads generated, pipeline influenced, deals closed.

The Three-Tier Measurement Model

Structure your measurement in three tiers, each with a different reporting cadence:

  • Tier 1 — Production metrics (weekly): Articles published, derivative assets created, time-to-publish per piece. Benchmark: AI-assisted production should hit 3–5x the output of purely human production at the same quality bar. If you're not hitting that, audit your brief quality and review cycle.
  • Tier 2 — Engagement metrics (monthly): Organic sessions by content cluster, average time on page, scroll depth, email click-through from newsletter sections. Segment by content type — long-form guides, repurposed social, email — to find which format drives qualified engagement.
  • Tier 3 — Revenue metrics (quarterly): Leads attributed to content (UTM-tagged + CRM attribution), pipeline influenced (contacts who read content before converting), and closed revenue from content-sourced leads. This requires clean UTM discipline and a CRM that tracks content touchpoints.

Setting Up Attribution in Your CRM

Every content URL must have a UTM convention. Use: utm_source=content&utm_medium=[format]&utm_campaign=[cluster]&utm_content=[slug]. Log the first-touch source and last-touch source separately in your CRM. Content almost never closes deals — it opens them. First-touch attribution shows content's prospecting power; multi-touch shows its deal-acceleration role.

Calculate content cost-per-lead by dividing total content production cost (AI tool subscriptions + human review hours × hourly rate) by leads generated in the same period. Compare this to paid channel CPL. Most teams discover content CPL is 60–80% lower than paid within 6 months of running a systematic AI content engine. That comparison, presented in a quarterly business review, is the proof point that unlocks larger content investment.
